policy_statement: Design stormwater projects to incorporate recreation, education, and transportation uses when feasible.
mechanism_description: Design stormwater projects to incorporate recreation, education, and transportation uses when feasible, while managing runoff in ways that reduce flooding risk.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavy precipitation) → stormwater system performance/overflow dynamics → ↓ flood extent and damages (↓ exposure/vulnerability)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: Municipal stormwater capital planning and design standards are adjusted to build multi-benefit projects (e.g., detention/retention features integrated with parks, trails, corridors) while meeting runoff control objectives.  
2) Mediating physical process: Increased storage/detention, infiltration/green infrastructure uptake, and/or improved conveyance reduces peak runoff rates, prevents sewer/storm drain surcharge, and limits surface ponding/overland flow during intense storms.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower probability and severity of pluvial flooding, reduced damage to assets and disruption to services under more frequent/intense rainfall events.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing flooding impacts from extreme rainfall by improving stormwater management capacity and performance.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Manage runoff to reduce flood risk from extreme precipitation—this is a hazard-impact reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some designs may add green space (potential minor carbon benefits) or improve water-use efficiency, the mechanism’s central and largest climate effect is reduced flooding exposure/vulnerability, i.e., adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented purely as green infrastructure for carbon sequestration or energy savings, categorization could shift; however, the stated mechanism explicitly targets runoff management and flood risk reduction, making Adaptation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that designs stormwater infrastructure for multi-benefits while managing runoff to reduce flooding shares the same primary flood-risk-reduction effect.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Co-benefits (recreation, education, active transport, urban greening) may support secondary tags, but those require policy-level specifics and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may rely heavily on vegetated/green infrastructure that also provides nature-based cooling or carbon sequestration; those are typically secondary to the primary flood-risk reduction unless the policy’s main objective is ecosystem enhancement or mitigation.
key_indicators: "Design stormwater projects"; "incorporate recreation, education, and transportation uses and facilities"; "when feasible"
co_benefits: Recreation amenities; educational facilities/programming; active transportation connectivity (trails/paths); public health/quality-of-life benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via capital project delivery (infrastructure-investment) or design standards (regulatory/performance) in practice, but the text itself is framed as a strategy-level design/scoping guidance without enforceable standards or funding commitments.
